one_liner: A 26-year-old man presents with 6 weeks of bloody diarrhea, lower abdominal cramping, and tenesmus. He reports 8–10 small-volume bloody bowel movements per day and 8 lb of weight loss.

stage2.hpi_extension: His stools contain frank blood and mucus, and he has urgency with a constant feeling of incomplete evacuation (**tenesmus**). Pain is crampy, lower abdominal, relieved by defecation. He also reports chronic upper right abdominal discomfort, itching, and occasional dark urine that prompted his PCP to check LFTs, which were elevated. He denies recent travel, antibiotic use, or sick contacts. Never smoked.
stage2.social_history: Accountant. Non-smoker (quit 1 year ago). Rare alcohol use.
stage2.physical_exam: Pale, thin. Sclera mildly icteric. Abdomen: diffuse lower abdominal tenderness without rebound or guarding. No masses, no hepatosplenomegaly. Rectal: **gross blood and mucus on the examining glove**, no perianal fistulas or fissures. Skin: no rashes. Joints: normal.

lab_results.result: Colonoscopy: **continuous, circumferential erythema, friability, and superficial ulcerations extending from the rectum to the mid-transverse colon without skip areas**. The terminal ileum is normal. Biopsy: **mucosal and submucosal inflammation limited to the mucosa with crypt abscesses and crypt distortion**; no granulomas.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Continuous inflammation starting at the rectum with crypt abscesses limited to the mucosa is diagnostic of ulcerative colitis and distinguishes it from Crohn disease.

lab_results.result: Alk phos 410 U/L (HIGH), GGT elevated, total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL (HIGH), AST/ALT mildly elevated. Albumin 3.1 g/dL (LOW). **p-ANCA positive** on reflex serology.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Cholestatic pattern of LFT elevation with p-ANCA positivity raises concern for concurrent primary sclerosing cholangitis, a classic extraintestinal association of UC.

lab_results.result: Abdominal radiograph: **loss of haustral markings in the descending colon ('lead pipe' appearance)**. MRCP: multifocal intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary strictures with a **'beaded' appearance**.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Lead pipe colon reflects chronic UC. Beaded biliary strictures on MRCP are characteristic of primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), which is strongly associated with UC.

stage5.final_clue: He is started on mesalamine and counseled about his markedly elevated lifetime risk of colorectal cancer (requiring surveillance colonoscopy starting 8 years after diagnosis) and his coexisting primary sclerosing cholangitis.

debrief.classic_presentation: Ulcerative colitis classically presents in a young adult (bimodal: 15–35 and 55–70) with bloody diarrhea, lower abdominal pain, tenesmus, urgency, and weight loss. Unlike Crohn disease, inflammation is limited to the colon, ALWAYS involves the rectum, and spreads continuously proximally. Extraintestinal features include primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), uveitis, pyoderma gangrenosum, erythema nodosum, and seronegative spondyloarthropathies.
debrief.pathophysiology: UC is a chronic relapsing inflammatory disease limited to the colonic mucosa and submucosa. Inflammation always begins at the rectum and extends proximally in a continuous, circumferential fashion. Histology shows crypt abscesses, crypt distortion, and superficial ulceration without granulomas. p-ANCA is present in ~70% of patients. Strong association with HLA-B27–linked spondyloarthropathies and primary sclerosing cholangitis. Paradoxically, smoking is protective.
debrief.treatment_overview: Induction: topical (suppository/enema) or oral 5-ASA agents (mesalamine, sulfasalazine) for mild-moderate disease; corticosteroids for flares; biologics (anti-TNF: infliximab) or cyclosporine for severe disease. Maintenance: 5-ASA, azathioprine, biologics. Colectomy is CURATIVE (unlike in Crohn) and indicated for refractory disease, dysplasia/cancer, or toxic megacolon. Colon cancer surveillance colonoscopy begins 8 years after diagnosis.
high_yield_pearls: UC = continuous, mucosal, rectum always involved, bloody diarrhea, crypt abscesses, lead pipe colon, p-ANCA positive. Colectomy is curative.
Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is strongly associated with UC — 'beaded' biliary strictures on MRCP, p-ANCA positive, elevated alk phos.
Toxic megacolon is a dreaded complication: colon dilation >6 cm with systemic toxicity. Avoid antidiarrheals and opioids in severe UC.
Smoking is PROTECTIVE in UC but WORSENS Crohn — classic USMLE pair.
Risk of colorectal cancer increases with duration and extent of disease; start surveillance colonoscopy 8 years after diagnosis.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Gastrointestinal chapter, 'Inflammatory bowel diseases — Ulcerative colitis' section.
